marriages.notes: Met Hattie in Nevada 1863 (four years after Comstock Lode discovery brought the Russell family to Carson City). She was 19; he was 37.

career.notes: Superintendent during the height of Comstock Lode silver mining boom; forced to resign following mental breakdown.

narrative_blocks.header: Suicide at Ophir 1883 left widow with four
narrative_blocks.body: After a series of business failures following his forced resignation from the Gould & Curry superintendency, Charles took his own life at Ophir CA on 9 February 1883 at age 56-57. His widow Hattie inherited the failing properties and would transform them into the Ranchito del Fuerte agricultural empire that made her one of America's first famous female inventors and conservationists.

narrative_blocks.header: Comstock boom superintendent who lost his grip
narrative_blocks.body: As superintendent of the Gould & Curry mine in Virginia City Nevada — one of the richest silver mines of the Comstock Lode era — he carried enormous financial and operational pressure. The strain triggered a mental breakdown that forced his resignation; he moved his family to Oakland to focus on rest, farming, and travel.

narrative_blocks.header: Met Hattie in Nevada four years after Comstock
narrative_blocks.body: The 1859 Comstock Lode silver discovery had lured the Russell family from Buffalo to Carson City Nevada by 1861. There the 37-year-old mining engineer met 19-year-old Hattie Russell, granddaughter of Col. Samuel Andrew Russell; they married in 1863.

sources.label: Find A Grave - Charles Lyman Strong
Mary Baker Eddy Library biographical research on Harriet Williams Russell Strong

sources.label: Find A Grave - Charles Lyman Strong
Mary Baker Eddy Library biographical research on Harriet Williams Russell Strong

narrative_blocks.header: Her 1887 dam patent informed Hoover Dam
narrative_blocks.body: In 1887 she patented a dam and reservoir system using a series of ascending dams, each braced by the water against the one below. Congress adopted that mechanism when it approved the Hoover Dam project in 1928.

narrative_blocks.header: Walnut Queen of Whittier within five years
narrative_blocks.body: Widowed at 39 with four daughters and a near-bankrupt ranch, she taught herself scientific agriculture by reading journals. Within five years she planted 150 acres of walnuts and became the largest commercial walnut grower in the United States.

narrative_blocks.header: I had the courage of ignorance and determination
narrative_blocks.body: Her husband Charles Lyman Strong had committed suicide after business failures, leaving her with four daughters and an unprofitable ranch near Whittier. Of her self-taught success she later said: 'I had the courage of ignorance and plenty of determination to back it up.'

narrative_blocks.header: Traveled with Susan B. Anthony for women's suffrage
narrative_blocks.body: She traveled with Susan B. Anthony promoting women's suffrage, hosting Anthony at her Rancho del Fuerte in 1895. She was the first woman member of the Los Angeles Chamber of Commerce and the first woman delegate to the U.S. Chamber of Commerce convention in 1918.

narrative_blocks.header: It was thought of by a woman
narrative_blocks.body: Appearing before Congress in 1918 to urge a series of Colorado River dams, she said: 'It was thought of by a woman.' Congress eventually built the Hoover Dam (1935) and the All-American Canal (1942) — both reflecting her ideas.

marriages.notes: Met in Nevada 1863, four years after the Comstock Lode discovery.
